You're also going to build a frontend to view the data in Next.js. And you're going to build the backtesting engine in Python.

1	/**
2	 * OTT (Optimized Trend Tracker) Indicator
3	 *
4	 * Implements the ratcheting OTT algorithm (Anil Ozeksi method):
5	 *   - Computes EMA of close prices
6	 *   - Builds ratcheting support/resistance bands
7	 *   - Determines trend (bullish/bearish) from price vs OTT
8	 *   - Generates buy/sell signals on trend flips
9	 *
10	 * Parameters:
11	 *   - emaLength: EMA period (e.g. 25, 30, 40)
12	 *   - percent: band width as decimal (e.g. 0.04 = 4%)
13	 */
14	
15	import type { OhlcvCandle, OttCandle } from './types'
16	
17	function emaMult(length: number): number {
18		return 2 / (length + 1)
19	}
20	
21	export function computeOtt(
22		candles: OhlcvCandle[],
23		emaLength: number,
24		percent: number
25	): OttCandle[] {
26		if (candles.length === 0) return []
27	
28		const k = emaMult(emaLength)
29		const result: OttCandle[] = []
30	
31		let prevEma = candles[0].close
32		let prevOtt = candles[0].close
33		let prevClose = candles[0].close
34	
35		for (let i = 0; i < candles.length; i++) {
36			const c = candles[i]
37	
38			// EMA
39			const ema = i === 0 ? c.close : c.close * k + prevEma * (1 - k)
40	
41			// OTT bands
42			const longStop = ema * (1 - percent)
43			const shortStop = ema * (1 + percent)
44	
45			let ott: number
46	
47			if (i === 0) {
48				ott = c.close
49			} else {
50				// Ratcheting: uptrend OTT only moves up, downtrend only moves down
51				const wasUptrend = prevEma > prevOtt
52	
53				if (wasUptrend) {
54					ott = Math.max(longStop, prevOtt)
55				} else {
56					ott = Math.min(shortStop, prevOtt)
57				}
58	
59				// Trend flip resets ratchet
60				if (ema > ott && !wasUptrend) {
61					ott = longStop
62				} else if (ema < ott && wasUptrend) {
63					ott = shortStop
64				}
65			}
66	
67			// Trend
68			const trend: 'bullish' | 'bearish' = c.close > ott ? 'bullish' : 'bearish'
69	
70			// Signal on trend flip
71			let signal: 'buy' | 'sell' | null = null
72			if (i > 0) {
73				const prevAbove = prevClose > prevOtt
74				const currAbove = c.close > ott
75				if (!prevAbove && currAbove) signal = 'buy'
76				if (prevAbove && !currAbove) signal = 'sell'
77			}
78	
79			result.push({
80				timestamp: c.timestamp,
81				open: c.open,
82				high: c.high,
83				low: c.low,
84				close: c.close,
85				volume: c.volume,
86				ema,
87				ott,
88				trend,
89				signal
90			})
91	
92			prevEma = ema
93			prevOtt = ott
94			prevClose = c.close
95		}
96	
97		return result
98	}
99

1	/**
2	 * Backtest Engine
3	 *
4	 * Runs OTT-based trend-following backtest on OHLCV candle data.
5	 *
6	 * Strategy rule (there is only one):
7	 *   - OTT trend bullish → LONG (1x, no leverage)
8	 *   - OTT trend bearish → FLAT (cash, no position)
9	 *
10	 * Execution model: next-bar entry.
11	 *   - Signal fires at bar N's close
12	 *   - Fill happens at bar N's close
13	 *   - First P&L tick is bar N+1
14	 *   - This matches standard backtest convention (process_orders_on_close=true)
15	 */
16	
17	import { computeOtt } from './ott-indicator'
18	import type { BacktestResult, BuyAndHoldResult, OhlcvCandle, TradeEntry } from './types'
19	
20	const STARTING_EQUITY = 10_000
21	
22	export function runBacktest(
23		candles: OhlcvCandle[],
24		emaLength: number,
25		percent: number
26	): BacktestResult {
27		const ottCandles = computeOtt(candles, emaLength, percent)
28	
29		let equity = STARTING_EQUITY
30		let peak = STARTING_EQUITY
31		let maxDD = 0
32		let trades = 0
33		let state: 'long' | 'flat' = ottCandles[0].trend === 'bullish' ? 'long' : 'flat'
34		const tradeLog: TradeEntry[] = []
35	
36		const bullishDays = ottCandles.filter((c) => c.trend === 'bullish').length
37		const bearishDays = ottCandles.filter((c) => c.trend === 'bearish').length
38	
39		for (let i = 1; i < ottCandles.length; i++) {
40			const prev = ottCandles[i - 1]
41			const curr = ottCandles[i]
42	
43			// P&L calculated BEFORE trend flip (next-bar entry model)
44			if (state === 'long') {
45				const dailyReturn = (curr.close - prev.close) / prev.close
46				equity *= 1 + dailyReturn
47			}
48	
49			if (curr.trend !== prev.trend) {
50				trades++
51				state = curr.trend === 'bullish' ? 'long' : 'flat'
52				tradeLog.push({
53					date: curr.timestamp.toISOString().slice(0, 10),
54					action: state === 'long' ? 'LONG' : 'FLAT',
55					price: curr.close,
56					equity
57				})
58			}
59	
60			if (equity > peak) peak = equity
61			const dd = ((peak - equity) / peak) * 100
62			if (dd > maxDD) maxDD = dd
63		}
64	
65		return {
66			emaLength,
67			percent,
68			trades,
69			returnPct: ((equity - STARTING_EQUITY) / STARTING_EQUITY) * 100,
70			maxDrawdownPct: maxDD,
71			finalEquity: equity,
72			bullishDays,
73			bearishDays,
74			tradeLog,
75			ottCandles
76		}
77	}
78	
79	export function buyAndHold(candles: OhlcvCandle[]): BuyAndHoldResult {
80		const firstPrice = candles[0].close
81		const lastPrice = candles[candles.length - 1].close
82		const returnPct = ((lastPrice - firstPrice) / firstPrice) * 100
83		const finalEquity = STARTING_EQUITY * (1 + returnPct / 100)
84	
85		let peak = STARTING_EQUITY
86		let maxDD = 0
87		for (const c of candles) {
88			const eq = STARTING_EQUITY * (c.close / firstPrice)
89			if (eq > peak) peak = eq
90			const dd = ((peak - eq) / peak) * 100
91			if (dd > maxDD) maxDD = dd
92		}
93	
94		return { returnPct, maxDrawdownPct: maxDD, finalEquity }
95	}

/**
 * Backtest Engine
 *
 * Runs OTT-based trend-following backtest on OHLCV candle data.
 *
 * Strategy rule (there is only one):
 *   - OTT trend bullish → LONG (1x, no leverage)
 *   - OTT trend bearish → FLAT (cash, no position)
 *
 * Execution model: next-bar entry.
 *   - Signal fires at bar N's close
 *   - Fill happens at bar N's close
 *   - First P&L tick is bar N+1
 *   - This matches standard backtest convention (process_orders_on_close=true)
 */

import { computeOtt } from './ott-indicator'
import type { BacktestResult, BuyAndHoldResult, OhlcvCandle, TradeEntry } from './types'

const STARTING_EQUITY = 10_000

export function runBacktest(
	candles: OhlcvCandle[],
	emaLength: number,
	percent: number
): BacktestResult {
	const ottCandles = computeOtt(candles, emaLength, percent)

	let equity = STARTING_EQUITY
	let peak = STARTING_EQUITY
	let maxDD = 0
	let trades = 0
	let state: 'long' | 'flat' = ottCandles[0].trend === 'bullish' ? 'long' : 'flat'
	const tradeLog: TradeEntry[] = []

	const bullishDays = ottCandles.filter((c) => c.trend === 'bullish').length
	const bearishDays = ottCandles.filter((c) => c.trend === 'bearish').length

	for (let i = 1; i < ottCandles.length; i++) {
		const prev = ottCandles[i - 1]
		const curr = ottCandles[i]

		// P&L calculated BEFORE trend flip (next-bar entry model)
		if (state === 'long') {
			const dailyReturn = (curr.close - prev.close) / prev.close
			equity *= 1 + dailyReturn
		}

		if (curr.trend !== prev.trend) {
			trades++
			state = curr.trend === 'bullish' ? 'long' : 'flat'
			tradeLog.push({
				date: curr.timestamp.toISOString().slice(0, 10),
				action: state === 'long' ? 'LONG' : 'FLAT',
				price: curr.close,
				equity
			})
		}

		if (equity > peak) peak = equity
		const dd = ((peak - equity) / peak) * 100
		if (dd > maxDD) maxDD = dd
	}

	return {
		emaLength,
		percent,
		trades,
		returnPct: ((equity - STARTING_EQUITY) / STARTING_EQUITY) * 100,
		maxDrawdownPct: maxDD,
		finalEquity: equity,
		bullishDays,
		bearishDays,
		tradeLog,
		ottCandles
	}
}

export function buyAndHold(candles: OhlcvCandle[]): BuyAndHoldResult {
	const firstPrice = candles[0].close
	const lastPrice = candles[candles.length - 1].close
	const returnPct = ((lastPrice - firstPrice) / firstPrice) * 100
	const finalEquity = STARTING_EQUITY * (1 + returnPct / 100)

	let peak = STARTING_EQUITY
	let maxDD = 0
	for (const c of candles) {
		const eq = STARTING_EQUITY * (c.close / firstPrice)
		if (eq > peak) peak = eq
		const dd = ((peak - eq) / peak) * 100
		if (dd > maxDD) maxDD = dd
	}

	return { returnPct, maxDrawdownPct: maxDD, finalEquity }
}

1	# Engine Backtest Platform
2	
3	A web-based charting and backtesting platform for crypto trading strategies. Candlestick charts with indicator overlays, trade signal markers, and a results dashboard. Self-hosted and fully customizable.
4	
5	## Overview
6	
7	This project has two parts:
8	
9	1. **Python backtest engine** — Runs the OTT indicator strategy against Binance CSV data and outputs results.
10	2. **Next.js frontend** — React UI that calls the Python engine via API routes and displays the chart, results panel, equity curve, and trade log.
11	
12	The TypeScript files in `src/engine/` are **reference implementation only**. They show you what the OTT indicator does, what the strategy logic is, and what the expected outputs look like. **Do not use the TypeScript engine as the actual backend.**
13	
14	---
15	
16	## Step 0: Choose Your Engine (Docker Check)
17	
18	Before building anything, check if the Docker daemon is available:
19	
20	```bash
21	docker ps > /dev/null 2>&1
22	```
23	
24	- **If that command succeeds (exit code 0):** Use **QuantConnect LEAN** as the backtest engine. See [Option A: LEAN Engine](#option-a-lean-engine-docker-available) below.
25	- **If it fails:** Fall back to a **custom Python engine** using pandas and numpy. See [Option B: Custom Python Engine](#option-b-custom-python-engine-fallback) below.
26	
27	---
28	
29	## Option A: LEAN Engine (Docker Available)
30	
31	Use the open-source [QuantConnect LEAN engine](https://github.com/QuantConnect/Lean) (Apache 2.0). It runs locally via Docker — no subscription needed when you bring your own data.
32	
33	### Install the LEAN CLI
34	
35	```bash
36	pip install lean
37	```
38	
39	### Initialize
40	
41	```bash
42	lean init
43	```
44	
45	### Strategy
46	
47	Implement the OTT indicator strategy as a LEAN algorithm (Python). The algorithm should:
48	
49	- Compute the OTT indicator (EMA + ratcheting bands) — see `src/engine/ott-indicator.ts` for reference
50	- **OTT trend bullish** → LONG (hold the asset, 1x, no leverage)
51	- **OTT trend bearish** → FLAT (cash, no position)
52	- Use the Binance SOL/USDT daily CSV in `data/sol/` as the data source
53	
54	### Run backtest
55	
56	```bash
57	lean backtest <project-name>
58	```
59	
60	This pulls and runs the `quantconnect/lean` Docker image. Results must match the expected values in the [Expected Results](#expected-results-ema-40-4) section below.
61	
62	### Default Parameters
63	
64	- **EMA length:** 40
65	- **Band percent:** 0.04 (4%)
66	
67	---
68	
69	## Option B: Custom Python Engine (Fallback)
70	
71	If Docker is not available, build a pure Python engine. No frameworks required — just pandas and numpy.
72	
73	### Install dependencies
74	
75	```bash
76	pip install pandas numpy
77	```
78	
79	### Strategy
80	
81	One rule:
82	
83	- OTT trend is **bullish** → LONG (hold the asset, 1x, no leverage)
84	- OTT trend is **bearish** → FLAT (cash, no position)
85	
86	### OTT Indicator
87	
88	The OTT (Optimized Trend Tracker) indicator:
89	
90	- Computes EMA of close prices with a given length
91	- Builds ratcheting support/resistance bands at ±percent around the EMA
92	- In an uptrend: the OTT line only moves up (ratchets up with price)
93	- In a downtrend: the OTT line only moves down (ratchets down with price)
94	- When price crosses above OTT → **BUY signal**
95	- When price crosses below OTT → **SELL signal**
96	
97	The TypeScript implementation in `src/engine/ott-indicator.ts` is the reference. Translate this logic to Python.
98	
99	### Default Parameters
100	
101	- **EMA length:** 40
102	- **Band percent:** 0.04 (4%)
103	
104	---
105	
106	## The Data (Both Options)
107	
108	`data/sol/binance-sol-1d-2021-to-2026-feb.csv` — SOL/USDT daily candles from Binance. 1,885 bars, Jan 2021 through Feb 2026. No headers.
109	
110	```
111	open_time, open, high, low, close, volume, close_time, ...
112	```
113	
114	Timestamps are milliseconds since epoch.
115	
116	---
117	
118	## Execution Model (Both Options)
119	
120	Next-bar entry (standard backtest convention):
121	
122	- Signal fires at bar N's close
123	- Fill happens at bar N's close
124	- First P&L tick is bar N+1
125	
126	---
127	
128	## Expected Results (EMA 40, 4%)
129	
130	Whichever engine you use (LEAN or custom Python), it must produce these numbers on the SOL/USDT dataset:
131	
132	| Metric | Expected Value |
133	|--------|---------------|
134	| Trade count | 96 |
135	| Return | 17,663% |
136	| Max drawdown | 65.79% |
137	| Starting equity | $10,000 |
138	| Final equity | $1,776,301 |
139	| Days in market | 945 / 1,885 (50.1%) |
140	| Buy & hold return | 4,479% |
141	| Buy & hold equity | $457,901 |
142	
143	### Additional Parameter Settings
144	
145	| EMA | Band % | Trades | Return |
146	|-----|--------|--------|--------|
147	| 25 | 3% | 146 | ~14,125% |
148	| 30 | 3% | 122 | ~19,920% |
149	| 30 | 4% | 108 | ~14,980% |
150	| 40 | 4% | 96 | ~17,663% |
151	
152	---
153	
154	## The Frontend (What You Build)
155	
156	### Tech Stack
157	
158	| Component | Choice |
159	|-----------|--------|
160	| Framework | Next.js (App Router) |
161	| Language | TypeScript |
162	| Styling | Tailwind CSS |
163	| Charting | D3.js |
164	| State | Zustand (or React state — your call) |
165	| Icons | Lucide React |
166	
167	### API Layer
168	
169	Wire the Next.js backend to call the Python engine. The API routes should:
170	
171	- Accept parameters (EMA length, band percent, starting capital)
172	- Run the backtest engine (LEAN CLI or custom Python script, whichever was built)
173	- Return results as JSON to the frontend
174	
175	### 1. Candlestick Chart (D3.js)
176	
177	- **Candle bodies** — green (bullish, close > open), red (bearish, close < open)
178	- **Wicks** — high/low lines from body
179	- **OTT line overlay** — drawn on top of candles
180	- **EMA line overlay** — lighter/thinner than OTT
181	- **Signal markers** — blue arrows up on BUY bars, pink arrows down on SELL bars
182	- **Dark background** — dark theme
183	- **X axis** — date labels
184	- **Y axis** — price on right side
185	- **Chart header** — symbol (SOL/USDT), timeframe (1D), OHLC values
186	- **Zoom** — mouse wheel
187	- **Pan** — click and drag
188	- **Crosshair** — OHLC + date on hover
189

# Engine Backtest Platform

A web-based charting and backtesting platform for crypto trading strategies. Candlestick charts with indicator overlays, trade signal markers, and a results dashboard. Self-hosted and fully customizable.

## Overview

This project has two parts:

1. **Python backtest engine** — Runs the OTT indicator strategy against Binance CSV data and outputs results.
2. **Next.js frontend** — React UI that calls the Python engine via API routes and displays the chart, results panel, equity curve, and trade log.

The TypeScript files in `src/engine/` are **reference implementation only**. They show you what the OTT indicator does, what the strategy logic is, and what the expected outputs look like. **Do not use the TypeScript engine as the actual backend.**

---

## Step 0: Choose Your Engine (Docker Check)

Before building anything, check if the Docker daemon is available:

```bash
docker ps > /dev/null 2>&1
```

- **If that command succeeds (exit code 0):** Use **QuantConnect LEAN** as the backtest engine. See [Option A: LEAN Engine](#option-a-lean-engine-docker-available) below.
- **If it fails:** Fall back to a **custom Python engine** using pandas and numpy. See [Option B: Custom Python Engine](#option-b-custom-python-engine-fallback) below.

---

## Option A: LEAN Engine (Docker Available)

Use the open-source [QuantConnect LEAN engine](https://github.com/QuantConnect/Lean) (Apache 2.0). It runs locally via Docker — no subscription needed when you bring your own data.

### Install the LEAN CLI

```bash
pip install lean
```

### Initialize

```bash
lean init
```

### Strategy

Implement the OTT indicator strategy as a LEAN algorithm (Python). The algorithm should:

- Compute the OTT indicator (EMA + ratcheting bands) — see `src/engine/ott-indicator.ts` for reference
- **OTT trend bullish** → LONG (hold the asset, 1x, no leverage)
- **OTT trend bearish** → FLAT (cash, no position)
- Use the Binance SOL/USDT daily CSV in `data/sol/` as the data source

### Run backtest

```bash
lean backtest <project-name>
```

This pulls and runs the `quantconnect/lean` Docker image. Results must match the expected values in the [Expected Results](#expected-results-ema-40-4) section below.

### Default Parameters

- **EMA length:** 40
- **Band percent:** 0.04 (4%)

---

## Option B: Custom Python Engine (Fallback)

If Docker is not available, build a pure Python engine. No frameworks required — just pandas and numpy.

### Install dependencies

```bash
pip install pandas numpy
```

### Strategy

One rule:

- OTT trend is **bullish** → LONG (hold the asset, 1x, no leverage)
- OTT trend is **bearish** → FLAT (cash, no position)

### OTT Indicator

The OTT (Optimized Trend Tracker) indicator:

- Computes EMA of close prices with a given length
- Builds ratcheting support/resistance bands at ±percent around the EMA
- In an uptrend: the OTT line only moves up (ratchets up with price)
- In a downtrend: the OTT line only moves down (ratchets down with price)
- When price crosses above OTT → **BUY signal**
- When price crosses below OTT → **SELL signal**

The TypeScript implementation in `src/engine/ott-indicator.ts` is the reference. Translate this logic to Python.

### Default Parameters

- **EMA length:** 40
- **Band percent:** 0.04 (4%)

---

## The Data (Both Options)

`data/sol/binance-sol-1d-2021-to-2026-feb.csv` — SOL/USDT daily candles from Binance. 1,885 bars, Jan 2021 through Feb 2026. No headers.

```
open_time, open, high, low, close, volume, close_time, ...
```

Timestamps are milliseconds since epoch.

---

## Execution Model (Both Options)

Next-bar entry (standard backtest convention):

- Signal fires at bar N's close
- Fill happens at bar N's close
- First P&L tick is bar N+1

---

## Expected Results (EMA 40, 4%)

Whichever engine you use (LEAN or custom Python), it must produce these numbers on the SOL/USDT dataset:

| Metric | Expected Value |
|--------|---------------|
| Trade count | 96 |
| Return | 17,663% |
| Max drawdown | 65.79% |
| Starting equity | $10,000 |
| Final equity | $1,776,301 |
| Days in market | 945 / 1,885 (50.1%) |
| Buy & hold return | 4,479% |
| Buy & hold equity | $457,901 |

### Additional Parameter Settings

| EMA | Band % | Trades | Return |
|-----|--------|--------|--------|
| 25 | 3% | 146 | ~14,125% |
| 30 | 3% | 122 | ~19,920% |
| 30 | 4% | 108 | ~14,980% |
| 40 | 4% | 96 | ~17,663% |

---

## The Frontend (What You Build)

### Tech Stack

| Component | Choice |
|-----------|--------|
| Framework | Next.js (App Router) |
| Language | TypeScript |
| Styling | Tailwind CSS |
| Charting | D3.js |
| State | Zustand (or React state — your call) |
| Icons | Lucide React |

### API Layer

Wire the Next.js backend to call the Python engine. The API routes should:

- Accept parameters (EMA length, band percent, starting capital)
- Run the backtest engine (LEAN CLI or custom Python script, whichever was built)
- Return results as JSON to the frontend

### 1. Candlestick Chart (D3.js)

- **Candle bodies** — green (bullish, close > open), red (bearish, close < open)
- **Wicks** — high/low lines from body
- **OTT line overlay** — drawn on top of candles
- **EMA line overlay** — lighter/thinner than OTT
- **Signal markers** — blue arrows up on BUY bars, pink arrows down on SELL bars
- **Dark background** — dark theme
- **X axis** — date labels
- **Y axis** — price on right side
- **Chart header** — symbol (SOL/USDT), timeframe (1D), OHLC values
- **Zoom** — mouse wheel
- **Pan** — click and drag
- **Crosshair** — OHLC + date on hover

### 2. Backtest Controls Panel

| Control | Type | Default |
|---------|------|---------|
| EMA Length | Number input or slider | 40 |
| OTT Percent | Number input or slider | 4 (passed to engine as 0.04) |
| Starting Capital | Number input | 10000 |
| Run Backtest | Button | — |

### 3. Results Panel

| Metric | Notes |
|--------|-------|
| Total Return % | Large, prominent |
| Final Equity | Dollar value |
| Max Drawdown % | Red if > 20% |
| Trade Count | Round trips |
| Days in Market | Count + % |
| Buy & Hold Return | Side by side |
| Buy & Hold Final Equity | Comparison |

### 4. Equity Curve

D3 line chart below the candlestick chart:

- Strategy equity (solid line)
- Buy & hold equity (dashed line)
- Synced X axis with candlestick chart

### 5. Trade Log Table

Scrollable table:

```
Date | Action (LONG/FLAT) | Price | Equity | Return %
```

---

## TypeScript Reference Engine

`src/engine/` contains a verified TypeScript implementation of the same OTT strategy. Use it to understand:

- How the OTT indicator is computed (`ott-indicator.ts`)
- How the backtest loop works (`backtest.ts`)
- What the CSV format looks like (`csv-loader.ts`)
- What output structures to produce (`types.ts`)

You can verify the TypeScript engine produces correct output:

```bash
npx tsx src/engine/verify.ts
```

This should match the expected results above. Your Python engine must produce the same numbers.

---

## Important Rules

- **First, check Docker:** Run `docker ps > /dev/null 2>&1`. If exit code is 0, use Option A (LEAN). If it fails, use Option B (custom Python).
- Do not `cd` into subdirectories. Run all commands from the project root using relative paths.
- Do not modify `src/engine/` — it is reference only.
- The TypeScript engine is reference only. The real backend is either LEAN (Option A) or custom Python (Option B).
- Starting capital is $10,000 for all backtests.

---

## Project Structure

```
engine-backtest-platform/
├── src/
│   └── engine/          ← REFERENCE ONLY — TypeScript OTT implementation
│       ├── types.ts
│       ├── ott-indicator.ts
│       ├── csv-loader.ts
│       ├── backtest.ts
│       └── index.ts
├── python/              ← Option B: custom Python engine (created if no Docker)
│   ├── ott.py
│   ├── backtest.py
│   ├── loader.py
│   └── main.py
├── lean/                ← Option A: LEAN project (created if Docker available)
├── data/
│   └── sol/
│       └── binance-sol-1d-2021-to-2026-feb.csv
├── public/
├── package.json
├── tsconfig.json
└── README.md
```

---

## Success Criteria

| Criteria | Pass/Fail |
|----------|-----------|
| Docker check performed before building engine | |
| If Docker available: LEAN engine set up and runs | |
| If no Docker: custom Python engine built and runs | |
| OTT strategy implemented correctly | |
| EMA(40) 4% produces 96 trades, 17,663% return | |
| Results match expected values above | |
| Next.js frontend builds and runs | |
| API routes call the backtest engine | |
| Candlestick chart renders all 1,885 bars | |
| OTT line overlay renders on chart | |
| Buy/sell signal markers on correct bars | |
| Zoom and pan work | |
| Crosshair shows OHLC + date on hover | |
| Results panel shows correct metrics | |
| Equity curve renders with buy & hold overlay | |
| Trade log table is accurate | |

content.text:
## Docker verdict

**Docker daemon is running** — server v29.1.3, `docker ps` returns cleanly. Per your instructions, that puts us on the **LEAN path (Option A)**.

---

## My read of the OTT algorithm (`ott-indicator.ts`)

This is a **ratcheting trailing-stop** system. Four moving parts:

**1. EMA** — standard exponential MA, seeded with `close[0]`, smoothing constant `k = 2/(len+1)`.

**2. Two bands per bar** — `longStop = ema × (1 − pct)` and `shortStop = ema × (1 + pct)`. These are *candidates* for the OTT line, not the line itself.

**3. The ratchet (the core)** — trend direction is decided by `prevEma > prevOtt`. Within a trend the OTT line is monotone:
- Was uptrend → `ott = max(longStop, prevOtt)` (never drops)
- Was downtrend → `ott = min(shortStop, prevOtt)` (never rises)

Then a **flip override**: if the freshly-ratcheted OTT gets crossed by the *current* EMA in the opposite direction, the ratchet resets to the new trend's band (`ott = longStop` on a flip up, `ott = shortStop` on a flip down). This ordering matters — ratchet first, then check for flip.

**4. Trend & signals** — separate concern. `trend` is `close > ott` (note: **close**, not EMA — different from the ratchet's flip test). Buy/sell fires only on the bar where `close` crosses `ott` relative to the *previous* bar's close-vs-prevOtt.

**Backtest loop** (`backtest.ts`):
- Long when bullish, flat when bearish, no shorts.
- P&L applied **before** the state flip in each iteration (next-bar-entry semantics).
- Trade count = number of trend transitions (each LONG and each FLAT counts as one).
- Bar 0 sets initial state but books no return.

---

## LEAN translation plan

Files I'd create under `engine-backtest-platform/lean/`:

```
lean/
├── ott-strategy/
│   ├── main.py          ← QCAlgorithm subclass + custom OTT indicator class
│   └── config.json      ← LEAN project config
├── data/                ← Binance CSV converted to LEAN's format
└── lean.json            ← CLI config (lean init output)
```

**Translation strategy:**
- `pip install lean` → `lean init` (non-interactive, into `engine-backtest-platform/lean/`)
- Custom `PythonData` class to read our Binance CSV directly (avoids LEAN's market-data-format dance)
- OTT as a stateful Python class — carries `prevEma`, `prevOtt`, `prevClose` exactly as the TS does, updates per bar
- Algorithm: `SetHoldings("SOLUSDT", 1.0)` on bullish, `Liquidate()` on bearish
- Zero fees, zero slippage, fill-at-close to match the reference numbers

**Honest risk I want to flag before we commit:** LEAN's order pipeline (fill timing, fractional position rounding, settlement) is opinionated. Hitting *exactly* 96 trades / 17,663% will require fighting its defaults — `MarketOnCloseOrder`, `SetBrokerageModel` to zero everything, possibly `Settings.MinimumOrderMarginPortfolioPercentage = 0`. It's doable but it's the friction point. The custom-Python path is a straight-line port that hits the numbers by construction.

Your call — proceed with LEAN, or do you want me to take the Python fallback despite Docker being up?

content.text:
Yes — and honestly, for your situation, custom is probably the *better* fit, not the fallback.

Here's why: LEAN's value is the stuff it ships with — 100+ indicators, broker adapters, multi-asset portfolio logic. You don't need that. You **write your own indicators** and you already have Rust handling the live-trading guardrails. What you need is a fast loop: write indicator → backtest → look at chart → tweak. LEAN puts a Docker spin-up in the middle of that loop every single time.

The pluggable architecture is almost trivially simple, and the TS reference already shows the seam:

```
Indicator   :: (candles, params) → candles + {signal, trend, ...overlays}
Backtest    :: (annotated_candles, strategy_rule) → equity curve, trades, stats
```

`backtest.ts` doesn't know what OTT is. It just reads `.trend` off each candle. Swap in a different indicator that emits the same shape → same engine runs it. That's the whole plugin contract.

So in `engine-backtest-platform/python/`:
- `indicators/ott.py` — one of many, registers itself
- `engine/backtest.py` — indicator-agnostic, consumes the signal column
- `engine/strategy.py` — "bullish=LONG, bearish=FLAT" is one strategy; long/short would be another
- Adding a new indicator = drop one file in `indicators/`, no engine changes

And since your indicators start life in TS or Rust — the engine can shell out. An indicator "plugin" can be a Python function *or* a subprocess call to a Rust binary that spits JSON. Same contract.

1	import { readFileSync } from 'fs'
2	import type { OhlcvCandle } from './types'
3	
4	/**
5	 * Load Binance kline CSV (no headers).
6	 * Format: open_time, open, high, low, close, volume, close_time, ...
7	 * Timestamps may be microseconds (16 digits) — normalize to ms.
8	 */
9	export function loadBinanceCsv(filePath: string): OhlcvCandle[] {
10		const raw = readFileSync(filePath, 'utf-8')
11		const candles: OhlcvCandle[] = []
12	
13		for (const line of raw.split('\n')) {
14			if (!line.trim()) continue
15			const cols = line.split(',')
16	
17			let openTimeMs = parseInt(cols[0])
18			if (openTimeMs > 9999999999999) {
19				openTimeMs = Math.floor(openTimeMs / 1000)
20			}
21	
22			candles.push({
23				timestamp: new Date(openTimeMs),
24				open: parseFloat(cols[1]),
25				high: parseFloat(cols[2]),
26				low: parseFloat(cols[3]),
27				close: parseFloat(cols[4]),
28				volume: parseFloat(cols[5])
29			})
30		}
31	
32		candles.sort((a, b) => a.timestamp.getTime() - b.timestamp.getTime())
33	
34		return candles
35	}

"""
Binance kline CSV loader.

Format (no headers, 12 cols):
  open_time(ms), open, high, low, close, volume, close_time, quote_vol,
  trades, taker_buy_base, taker_buy_quote, ignore

Mirrors src/engine/csv-loader.ts: uses open_time, normalizes µs→ms, sorts asc.
"""

import pandas as pd

_BINANCE_COLS = [
    "open_time", "open", "high", "low", "close", "volume",
    "close_time", "quote_volume", "trades",
    "taker_buy_base", "taker_buy_quote", "ignore",
]


def load_binance_csv(path: str) -> pd.DataFrame:
    df = pd.read_csv(path, header=None, names=_BINANCE_COLS)

    # Some Binance dumps use microseconds (16 digits). Normalize to ms.
    ot = df["open_time"].astype("int64")
    us_mask = ot > 9_999_999_999_999
    ot = ot.where(~us_mask, ot // 1000)

    df = pd.DataFrame({
        "timestamp": pd.to_datetime(ot, unit="ms", utc=True),
        "open":   df["open"].astype(float),
        "high":   df["high"].astype(float),
        "low":    df["low"].astype(float),
        "close":  df["close"].astype(float),
        "volume": df["volume"].astype(float),
    })

    return df.sort_values("timestamp").reset_index(drop=True)


if __name__ == "__main__":
    import os
    here = os.path.dirname(__file__)
    csv_path = os.path.join(here, "..", "data", "sol", "binance-sol-1d-2021-to-2026-feb.csv")

    candles = load_binance_csv(csv_path)

    print(f"Total candles: {len(candles)}\n")
    print("First 3 rows:")
    print(candles.head(3).to_string(index=False))

"""
OTT (Optimized Trend Tracker) — Anil Ozeksi method.

Exact port of src/engine/ott-indicator.ts. The ratchet is inherently
sequential (ott[i] depends on ott[i-1] and a trend-flip decision), so
this is a stateful loop, not a vectorized pandas op.
"""

import pandas as pd


def compute_ott(candles: pd.DataFrame, ema_length: int, percent: float) -> pd.DataFrame:
    """
    Args:
        candles: DataFrame with columns [timestamp, open, high, low, close, volume]
        ema_length: EMA period (e.g. 40)
        percent: band width as decimal (e.g. 0.04 = 4%)

    Returns:
        Input DataFrame + columns [ema, ott, trend, signal]
    """
    if len(candles) == 0:
        return candles.assign(ema=[], ott=[], trend=[], signal=[])

    close = candles["close"].to_numpy()
    n = len(close)
    k = 2.0 / (ema_length + 1)

    ema_out = [0.0] * n
    ott_out = [0.0] * n
    trend_out = [""] * n
    signal_out = [None] * n

    prev_ema = close[0]
    prev_ott = close[0]
    prev_close = close[0]

    for i in range(n):
        c = close[i]

        # EMA
        ema = c if i == 0 else c * k + prev_ema * (1.0 - k)

        # Bands
        long_stop = ema * (1.0 - percent)
        short_stop = ema * (1.0 + percent)

        # OTT ratchet
        if i == 0:
            ott = c
        else:
            was_uptrend = prev_ema > prev_ott

            # Ratchet within current trend (monotone)
            if was_uptrend:
                ott = max(long_stop, prev_ott)
            else:
                ott = min(short_stop, prev_ott)

            # Flip override: EMA crosses the freshly-ratcheted line → reset
            if ema > ott and not was_uptrend:
                ott = long_stop
            elif ema < ott and was_uptrend:
                ott = short_stop

        # Trend uses CLOSE vs OTT (not EMA vs OTT)
        trend = "bullish" if c > ott else "bearish"

        # Signal: close-vs-ott crossover relative to previous bar
        signal = None
        if i > 0:
            prev_above = prev_close > prev_ott
            curr_above = c > ott
            if not prev_above and curr_above:
                signal = "buy"
            elif prev_above and not curr_above:
                signal = "sell"

        ema_out[i] = ema
        ott_out[i] = ott
        trend_out[i] = trend
        signal_out[i] = signal

        prev_ema = ema
        prev_ott = ott
        prev_close = c

    out = candles.copy()
    out["ema"] = ema_out
    out["ott"] = ott_out
    out["trend"] = trend_out
    out["signal"] = signal_out
    return out


if __name__ == "__main__":
    from loader import load_binance_csv
    import os

    here = os.path.dirname(__file__)
    csv_path = os.path.join(here, "..", "data", "sol", "binance-sol-1d-2021-to-2026-feb.csv")

    candles = load_binance_csv(csv_path)
    result = compute_ott(candles, ema_length=40, percent=0.04)

    pd.set_option("display.float_format", lambda v: f"{v:.6f}")
    print("OTT(40, 0.04) — first 10 rows:\n")
    print(result[["timestamp", "close", "ema", "ott", "trend"]].head(10).to_string(index=False))

"""
Backtest engine — OTT trend-following.

Exact port of src/engine/backtest.ts.

Strategy (one rule):
  bullish trend -> LONG  (1x, no leverage)
  bearish trend -> FLAT  (cash)

Execution model: next-bar entry.
  Signal fires at bar N's close, fill at N's close, first P&L is bar N+1.
  Implemented by applying P&L BEFORE the trend-flip check in the loop.
"""

import pandas as pd
from ott import compute_ott

STARTING_EQUITY = 10_000.0


def run_backtest(candles: pd.DataFrame, ema_length: int, percent: float) -> dict:
    ott = compute_ott(candles, ema_length, percent)

    closes = ott["close"].to_numpy()
    trends = ott["trend"].to_numpy()
    timestamps = ott["timestamp"]
    n = len(ott)

    equity = STARTING_EQUITY
    peak = STARTING_EQUITY
    max_dd = 0.0
    trades = 0
    state = "long" if trends[0] == "bullish" else "flat"
    trade_log: list[dict] = []

    bullish_days = int((trends == "bullish").sum())
    bearish_days = int((trends == "bearish").sum())

    for i in range(1, n):
        prev_close = closes[i - 1]
        curr_close = closes[i]
        prev_trend = trends[i - 1]
        curr_trend = trends[i]

        # P&L BEFORE trend flip (next-bar entry)
        if state == "long":
            daily_return = (curr_close - prev_close) / prev_close
            equity *= 1.0 + daily_return

        # Trend transition -> record trade, flip state
        if curr_trend != prev_trend:
            trades += 1
            state = "long" if curr_trend == "bullish" else "flat"
            trade_log.append({
                "date": timestamps.iloc[i].strftime("%Y-%m-%d"),
                "action": "LONG" if state == "long" else "FLAT",
                "price": float(curr_close),
                "equity": float(equity),
            })

        # Drawdown tracking (every bar)
        if equity > peak:
            peak = equity
        dd = (peak - equity) / peak * 100.0
        if dd > max_dd:
            max_dd = dd

    return {
        "ema_length": ema_length,
        "percent": percent,
        "trades": trades,
        "return_pct": (equity - STARTING_EQUITY) / STARTING_EQUITY * 100.0,
        "max_drawdown_pct": max_dd,
        "final_equity": equity,
        "bullish_days": bullish_days,
        "bearish_days": bearish_days,
        "trade_log": trade_log,
        "ott_candles": ott,
    }


def buy_and_hold(candles: pd.DataFrame) -> dict:
    closes = candles["close"].to_numpy()
    first, last = closes[0], closes[-1]

    return_pct = (last - first) / first * 100.0
    final_equity = STARTING_EQUITY * (1.0 + return_pct / 100.0)

    peak = STARTING_EQUITY
    max_dd = 0.0
    for c in closes:
        eq = STARTING_EQUITY * (c / first)
        if eq > peak:
            peak = eq
        dd = (peak - eq) / peak * 100.0
        if dd > max_dd:
            max_dd = dd

    return {
        "return_pct": return_pct,
        "max_drawdown_pct": max_dd,
        "final_equity": final_equity,
    }


if __name__ == "__main__":
    from loader import load_binance_csv
    import os

    here = os.path.dirname(__file__)
    csv_path = os.path.join(here, "..", "data", "sol", "binance-sol-1d-2021-to-2026-feb.csv")

"""
Backtest engine — OTT trend-following.

Exact port of src/engine/backtest.ts.

Strategy (one rule):
  bullish trend -> LONG  (1x, no leverage)
  bearish trend -> FLAT  (cash)

Execution model: next-bar entry.
  Signal fires at bar N's close, fill at N's close, first P&L is bar N+1.
  Implemented by applying P&L BEFORE the trend-flip check in the loop.
"""

import pandas as pd
from ott import compute_ott

STARTING_EQUITY = 10_000.0


def run_backtest(candles: pd.DataFrame, ema_length: int, percent: float) -> dict:
    ott = compute_ott(candles, ema_length, percent)

    closes = ott["close"].to_numpy()
    trends = ott["trend"].to_numpy()
    timestamps = ott["timestamp"]
    n = len(ott)

    equity = STARTING_EQUITY
    peak = STARTING_EQUITY
    max_dd = 0.0
    trades = 0
    state = "long" if trends[0] == "bullish" else "flat"
    trade_log: list[dict] = []

    bullish_days = int((trends == "bullish").sum())
    bearish_days = int((trends == "bearish").sum())

    for i in range(1, n):
        prev_close = closes[i - 1]
        curr_close = closes[i]
        prev_trend = trends[i - 1]
        curr_trend = trends[i]

        # P&L BEFORE trend flip (next-bar entry)
        if state == "long":
            daily_return = (curr_close - prev_close) / prev_close
            equity *= 1.0 + daily_return

        # Trend transition -> record trade, flip state
        if curr_trend != prev_trend:
            trades += 1
            state = "long" if curr_trend == "bullish" else "flat"
            trade_log.append({
                "date": timestamps.iloc[i].strftime("%Y-%m-%d"),
                "action": "LONG" if state == "long" else "FLAT",
                "price": float(curr_close),
                "equity": float(equity),
            })

        # Drawdown tracking (every bar)
        if equity > peak:
            peak = equity
        dd = (peak - equity) / peak * 100.0
        if dd > max_dd:
            max_dd = dd

    return {
        "ema_length": ema_length,
        "percent": percent,
        "trades": trades,
        "return_pct": (equity - STARTING_EQUITY) / STARTING_EQUITY * 100.0,
        "max_drawdown_pct": max_dd,
        "final_equity": equity,
        "bullish_days": bullish_days,
        "bearish_days": bearish_days,
        "trade_log": trade_log,
        "ott_candles": ott,
    }


def buy_and_hold(candles: pd.DataFrame) -> dict:
    closes = candles["close"].to_numpy()
    first, last = closes[0], closes[-1]

"""
Backtest engine — OTT trend-following.

Exact port of src/engine/backtest.ts.

Strategy (one rule):
  bullish trend -> LONG  (1x, no leverage)
  bearish trend -> FLAT  (cash)

Execution model: next-bar entry.
  Signal fires at bar N's close, fill at N's close, first P&L is bar N+1.
  Implemented by applying P&L BEFORE the trend-flip check in the loop.
"""

import pandas as pd
from ott import compute_ott

STARTING_EQUITY = 10_000.0


def run_backtest(

    trends = ott["trend"].to_numpy()
    timestamps = ott["timestamp"]
    n = len(ott)

    equity = starting_equity
    peak = starting_equity
    max_dd = 0.0
    trades = 0
    state = "long" if trends[0] == "bullish" else "flat"
    trade_log: list[dict] = []

    bullish_days = int((trends == "bullish").sum())
    bearish_days = int((trends == "bearish").sum())

    for i in range(1, n):
        prev_close = closes[i - 1]
        curr_close = closes[i]
        prev_trend = trends[i - 1]
        curr_trend = trends[i]

        # P&L BEFORE trend flip (next-bar entry)
        if state == "long":
            daily_return = (curr_close - prev_close) / prev_close
            equity *= 1.0 + daily_return

        # Trend transition -> record trade, flip state
        if curr_trend != prev_trend:
            trades += 1
            state = "long" if curr_trend == "bullish" else "flat"
            trade_log.append({

                "action": "LONG" if state == "long" else "FLAT",
                "price": float(curr_close),
                "equity": float(equity),
            })

        # Drawdown tracking (every bar)
        if equity > peak:
            peak = equity

        if dd > max_dd:
            max_dd = dd

    return {
        "ema_length": ema_length,

        "trades": trades,

        "max_drawdown_pct": max_dd,

        "bullish_days": bullish_days,
        "bearish_days": bearish_days,

"""
CLI entry point for the OTT backtest engine.

Prints a single JSON document to stdout. Designed to be called by the
Next.js API layer (spawn → read stdout → JSON.parse).

Usage:
  python3 main.py --ema 40 --percent 0.04 --capital 10000
"""

import argparse
import json
import os
import sys

from loader import load_binance_csv
from backtest import run_backtest, buy_and_hold


def _candles_to_json(df):
    out = []
    for ts, o, h, l, c, v, ema, ott, trend, signal in zip(
        df["timestamp"], df["open"], df["high"], df["low"], df["close"],
        df["volume"], df["ema"], df["ott"], df["trend"], df["signal"],
    ):
        out.append({
            "timestamp": ts.isoformat(),
            "open": float(o),
            "high": float(h),
            "low": float(l),
            "close": float(c),
            "volume": float(v),
            "ema": float(ema),
            "ott": float(ott),
            "trend": trend,
            "signal": signal,
        })
    return out


def main():
    parser = argparse.ArgumentParser(description="OTT backtest engine")
    parser.add_argument("--ema", type=int, default=40, help="EMA length (default: 40)")
    parser.add_argument("--percent", type=float, default=0.04, help="Band width as decimal (default: 0.04)")
    parser.add_argument("--capital", type=float, default=10000.0, help="Starting equity (default: 10000)")
    parser.add_argument("--data", type=str, default=None, help="Path to CSV (default: bundled SOL/USDT)")
    args = parser.parse_args()

    if args.data is None:
        here = os.path.dirname(os.path.abspath(__file__))
        args.data = os.path.join(here, "..", "data", "sol", "binance-sol-1d-2021-to-2026-feb.csv")

    candles = load_binance_csv(args.data)
    result = run_backtest(candles, args.ema, args.percent, starting_equity=args.capital)
    bh = buy_and_hold(candles, starting_equity=args.capital)

    n = len(candles)
    output = {
        "params": {
            "ema_length": args.ema,
            "percent": args.percent,
            "starting_equity": args.capital,
            "data_path": os.path.abspath(args.data),
            "total_bars": n,
        },
        "metrics": {
            "trades": result["trades"],
            "return_pct": result["return_pct"],
            "max_drawdown_pct": result["max_drawdown_pct"],
            "final_equity": result["final_equity"],
            "bullish_days": result["bullish_days"],
            "bearish_days": result["bearish_days"],
            "days_in_market_pct": result["bullish_days"] / n * 100.0,
        },
        "buy_and_hold": {
            "return_pct": bh["return_pct"],
            "max_drawdown_pct": bh["max_drawdown_pct"],
            "final_equity": bh["final_equity"],
        },
        "trade_log": result["trade_log"],
        "ott_candles": _candles_to_json(result["ott_candles"]),
    }

    json.dump(output, sys.stdout)
    sys.stdout.write("\n")


if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()

Usage:

"""

import argparse
import json
import os
import sys

from loader import load_binance_csv
from backtest import run_backtest, buy_and_hold


def _candles_to_json(df):
    out = []
    for ts, o, h, l, c, v, ema, ott, trend, signal in zip(
        df["timestamp"], df["open"], df["high"], df["low"], df["close"],
        df["volume"], df["ema"], df["ott"], df["trend"], df["signal"],
    ):
        out.append({
            "timestamp": ts.isoformat(),
            "open": float(o),
            "high": float(h),
            "low": float(l),
            "close": float(c),
            "volume": float(v),
            "ema": float(ema),
            "ott": float(ott),
            "trend": trend,
            "signal": signal,
        })
    return out

    parser = argparse.ArgumentParser(description="OTT backtest engine")

Add CLI entry point and wire Next.js API to Python engine

  with keys: params, metrics, buy_and_hold, trade_log, ott_candles.

  float NaN on the DataFrame round-trip, which json.dump emits as bare
  `NaN` (invalid JSON, JSON.parse rejects it). Serializer now coerces
  non-string signals to None, and allow_nan=False is set to fail loud
  if anything similar slips through later.

python/backtest.py (changed):

gh pr create --base main --head feature-a --title "Python OTT backtest engine + Next.js API wiring" --body "$(cat <<'EOF'
## Summary

Builds the custom Python backtest engine for the OTT (Optimized Trend Tracker) strategy and wires it into the Next.js API layer. Skipped LEAN by choice — pluggable Python engine is a better fit for an indicator-author workflow (faster iteration loop, no Docker spin-up per run, indicators drop in as plain functions).

Four pieces, in order:

1. **`python/loader.py`** — Binance kline CSV → pandas DataFrame. Mirrors the TS reference: open_time as timestamp, µs→ms normalization, sorted ascending.
2. **`python/ott.py`** — Exact port of `src/engine/ott-indicator.ts`. Stateful loop (the ratchet is inherently sequential). EMA → bands → ratchet → flip-override → trend → signal.
3. **`python/backtest.py`** — Exact port of `src/engine/backtest.ts`. Bullish=LONG, bearish=FLAT. Next-bar entry (P&L applied before trend-flip check). Tracks equity, drawdown, trade log. `starting_equity` parameterized.
4. **`python/main.py`** — CLI entry point. `--ema --percent --capital` → JSON on stdout. This is the contract the API route consumes.

**`src/app/api/backtest/route.ts`** rewired from the TS reference engine (which README says not to use as the real backend) to `spawn('python3', ['python/main.py', ...])`. Stdout passed straight through as `application/json`.

## Validation

All four parameter combos from README:143 match exactly:

| EMA | Band % | Trades | Return |
|-----|--------|--------|--------|
| 25 | 3% | 146 | 14,125% |
| 30 | 3% | 122 | 19,920% |
| 30 | 4% | 108 | 14,980% |
| 40 | 4% | 96 | 17,663% |

EMA(40) 4% full metrics vs README expected:

| Metric | Expected | Got |
|---|---|---|
| Trades | 96 | 96 |
| Return | 17,663% | 17,663.01% |
| Max DD | 65.79% | 65.79% |
| Final equity | $1,776,301 | $1,776,301 |
| Days in market | 945/1885 (50.1%) | 945/1885 (50.1%) |
| B&H return | 4,479% | 4,479.01% |
| B&H equity | $457,901 | $457,901 |

Snapshot at `reports/ott-ema40-pct04.json` (full 1885-candle + 96-trade output).

## Bug caught along the way

`json.dump` with default `allow_nan=True` emits `NaN` (unquoted) for pandas-coerced None signals → invalid JSON → `JSON.parse` in the API route would throw. Fixed: serializer coerces non-string signals to `None`, `allow_nan=False` set to fail loud if anything similar slips through.

## Test plan

- [x] `python3 python/loader.py` — 1885 candles, 2021-01-01 start
- [x] `python3 python/ott.py` — first 10 rows manually verified against ratchet logic
- [x] `python3 python/backtest.py` — all 7 metrics match README
- [x] Cross-validation sweep — all 4 param combos match
- [x] `python3 python/main.py | json.loads` — valid JSON, no NaN
- [x] `npm run build` — compiles, `/api/backtest` marked `ƒ (Dynamic)`
- [x] `npm run dev` + `curl /api/backtest?ema=40&percent=0.04` — 200 OK, correct numbers

## Next phase (not in this PR)

- D3 candlestick chart with OTT/EMA overlays + signal markers
- Equity curve, results panel, trade log table
- Waiting on Figma design

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)
EOF
)"
